title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of photography. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Capturing Time: The Birth of the Image
content.text: The word 'photography' literally means 'drawing with light,' a concept that has fascinated thinkers since the time of Aristotle. However, the first successful attempt to permanently capture an image did not occur until 1826. Joseph Nicéphore Niépce used a sliding wooden box and a plate coated with bitumen to record the view from his window in France. ||(41)|| [41]This cumbersome process made it impossible to photograph people or moving objects[/41]. It was Louis Daguerre who took Niépce’s work and refined it into a commercially viable product. His 'Daguerreotype' reduced the exposure time to just a few minutes, turning photography from a laboratory experiment into a societal phenomenon. ||(42)|| [42]These silver-plated copper sheets were incredibly detailed, but they had a major flaw: they were unique objects[/42]. There was no way to create copies of a Daguerreotype. This changed with the invention of the 'calotype' by William Henry Fox Talbot, which used paper negatives to allow for multiple prints. ||(43)|| [43]As the technology became more portable, photographers began to venture out of the studio[/43]. They traveled to battlefields, distant colonies, and the rapidly growing slums of industrial cities. For the first time, the public could see the 'unfiltered truth' of the world beyond their own neighborhoods. ||(44)|| [44]This democratic shift was accelerated by George Eastman and the introduction of the Kodak camera[/44]. With the slogan 'You press the button, we do the rest,' Eastman removed the need for complex chemicals and heavy equipment. Suddenly, photography was no longer the exclusive domain of professionals; it belonged to the masses. ||(45)|| [45]The arrival of digital sensors in the late 20th century completed this transformation[/45]. Images became intangible data, capable of being shared instantly across the globe. The physical constraints of film and processing disappeared, leading to the current era where billions of photos are taken every single day. ||(46)|| [46]While the tools have changed from wooden boxes to smartphones, the fundamental impulse remains the same[/46]. We still seek to freeze a moment in time, to bear witness to our lives, and to share our perspective with the world. Photography remains our most powerful tool for memory and storytelling.

paragraphs.A: Despite [41] the historical importance of this first photo [/41], it was far from practical. The exposure time lasted a staggering eight hours, requiring the sun to move across the sky, which resulted in the buildings being lit from both sides in the final image.
paragraphs.B: The [42] popularity of these early portraits [/42] was immense. Middle-class families flocked to studios to have their likenesses captured, viewing the sharp, mirror-like images as a way to achieve a kind of immortality that was previously reserved for the aristocracy.
paragraphs.C: This [43] shift toward reproducibility [/43] laid the foundation for modern photography. The ability to produce an infinite number of images from a single negative meant that photos could be used in newspapers, books, and advertisements, reaching a mass audience for the first time.
paragraphs.D: Such [44] documentary power [/44] gave photography a moral weight. Images of the American Civil War or the Crimean conflict brought the horrors of combat into the comfort of the Victorian living room, forever changing the way societies viewed political and social issues.
paragraphs.E: However, [45] this accessibility came with a price [/45]. As cameras became ubiquitous, the artistic prestige of the photograph began to be questioned. Critics argued that if anyone could take a picture, the skill and vision of the individual photographer were being devalued by the machine.
paragraphs.F: This [46] saturation of images [/46] has created a new set of challenges for the modern age. In a world where every moment is recorded and uploaded, we must grapple with issues of privacy, truth, and the overwhelming volume of visual information that competes for our attention.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] artists initially feared [/47] that photography would mean the death of painting. They believed that a machine that could capture reality with such precision would make the traditional skills of the brush and canvas obsolete, though the two mediums eventually learned to coexist.

questions.explanation: A describes the 'first photo' mentioned before the gap and explains why the 'eight-hour exposure' made it 'far from practical' for the reasons given in the text.
B refers to the 'popularity of these early portraits' (the Daguerreotypes) and explains how they became a 'societal phenomenon' among the middle class.
C links the 'shift toward reproducibility' to the 'multiple prints' enabled by the calotype mentioned just before the gap.
D discusses the 'documentary power' of images from 'battlefields' and 'slums', linking to the public's new ability to see the 'unfiltered truth'.
E explains the 'price' of 'accessibility' (the devaluing of skill) following the introduction of the mass-market Kodak camera.
F refers to the 'saturation of images' (billions taken every day) and the modern challenges of 'digital sensors' and instant sharing.
